Biostatistics

Contactpranav.joshi@alumni.iitgn.ac.in
CreatorPranav Joshi
Sensitivity and Specficity

In a screening test, we expect the majority of population to not have a disease.

In a diagnostic test, we expect the majority of population to have the disease.

Let D+,D−D^+,D^-D+,D− denote for a person to have a disease or not, and let T+,T−T^+,T^-T+,T− be test results.

“Sensitivity” of the test is P(T+∣D+)P(T^+|D^+)P(T+∣D+)

“Specificity” of the test is P(T−∣D−)P(T^-|D^-)P(T−∣D−)

When we say “probability of false positive” , we mean P(T+∣D−)P(T^+|D^-)P(T+∣D−). Similar case with false negative rate.

What we want to find in a clinical setting is actually P(D+∣T+)P(D^+|T^+)P(D+∣T+) .

We can compute this from the sensitivity as

All the terms on the right can be computed from the data.

The term P(D+)P(D^+)P(D+) is called the “prevelance” . This term can be calculated only when there’s another test T′T'T′ which has 100% sensitivity.

Once can expand the denominator as

Thus, we can write

The LHS, namely P(D+∣T+)P(D^+|T^+)P(D+∣T+) is called the “Positive predictive value” .

Similarly, P(D−∣T−)P(D^-|T^-)P(D−∣T−) is called the “Negative predictive value” .

  • Question

    Suppose in a population P(D+∣T+)=34.5%P(D^+|T^+) = 34.5\%P(D+∣T+)=34.5% for a covid test and P(D+)=5%P(D^+) = 5\%P(D+)=5% , Thus, the probability of covid increases by a factor of 6.9 when the individual tests positive.

In order to remove P(D+)P(D^+)P(D+) from calculations, you can instead use the odds ratio. Namely, O(D+∣T+)=P(D+∣T+)/P(D−∣T+)O(D^+|T^+) = P(D^+|T^+) / P(D^-|T^+)O(D+∣T+)=P(D+∣T+)/P(D−∣T+) .Using bayes rule for both numerator and denominator, we get

So, the factor by which your odds of disease increase on testing are now only dependent on the test metrics and not the metrics of the population.

If the ratio is 1, then the test is useless. With some very simple algebra, we can get from that condition.

Due to common sense reasons, no one would ever come up with a test having sens/(1−spec)<1\text{sens}/(1-\text{spec}) < 1sens/(1−spec)<1 since you can just use ¬T\lnot T¬T as a good test at that point.

Rates and Standardisation
  • Demographic data
    • size of population
    • composition by sex and age.
  • Vital statistics
    • births, deaths
    • marriages, divorces
    • occurence of disease
  • A rate is the number of cases divided by the population (for a fixed time period). Note that each participant can have multiple cases.
  • A proportion is fraction of participants who have the disease. This is basically risk .
  • incidence rate is number of cases divided by the person time.
  • Odds of disease in a particular time frame is risk/(1−risk)\text{risk}/(1-\text{risk})risk/(1−risk)
  • death rate (or mortality rate) is number of deaths over the size of population at risk (all individuals eligible to be part of survey).
  • mortality rate is often expressed as number of deaths per 1000 people
  • perinatal death rate is number of stillbirths (dead on birth) plus the number of deaths in the first 7 days divided by total number of births (still or live)
  • age specific mortality rate are death rates for a particular demographic in terms of age
  • Confounder

    A “confounder” is a factor that the adjusted rates depend on . For example, age is a confounder of age specific mortality rate.

    So, if you want to see the effects of health services on mortality rate, then you need your experiment and constrol group to be from the same demographic. You can’t have kids in one and grandparents in other.
    For example, if you study the mortality rate over time and see it increasing even though healthcare is getting better, it might be because the population is slowly getting concentrated towards old age (aging of the population as a whole).

  • Direct Standardisation.

    Suppose the distribution of the demographic into age groups G1,G2,G3G_1,G_2,G_3G1​,G2​,G3​ is w1,w2,w3w_1,w_2,w_3w1​,w2​,w3​ in year t0t_0t0​ and w1′,w2′,w3′w_1',w_2',w_3'w1′​,w2′​,w3′​ in year t1t_1t1​ , and the group specific disease rates (or mortality rates) are x1,x2,x3x_1,x_2,x_3x1​,x2​,x3​ in t0t_0t0​ and x1′,x2′,x3′x_1',x_2',x_3'x1′​,x2′​,x3′​ in ttt .

    Then, the standardised rate for t1t_1t1​ is w1x1′+w2x2′+w3x3′w_1x_1'+w_2x_2'+w_3x_3'w1​x1′​+w2​x2′​+w3​x3′​ , that is to say, the rate you would get with the same demographic distribution as it was in t0t_0t0​ .

    This is a better thing to compare, rather than the actual rate.

  • Indirect Standardisation

    For a group whose demographic distribution you know, you can use the standard rates (for the whole country or something) to get an expected value of the rate for the whole group as ∑iwixi,stand\sum_i w_i x_{i,stand}∑i​wi​xi,stand​ .

    This is the indirectly standardised rate. Then, if you get the actual rate for that population (which only needs you to know total number of cases and total number of participants), you can compute the “Standard Mortality Ratio” (SMR) by dividing with the expected rate.

Notation

Given data XiX_iXi​ , define

  • average Xˉ=1n∑iXi\bar X = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i X_iXˉ=n1​∑i​Xi​
  • sample standard deviation s=1n−1∑i(Xi−Xˉ)2s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i (X_i - \bar X)^2}s=n−11​∑i​(Xi​−Xˉ)2​
  • population mean μ\muμ
  • population standard deviation σ\sigmaσ

Given data Xi,YiX_i,Y_iXi​,Yi​ define

  • Sample Pearson correlation r=1n−1∑i(Xi−Xˉ)sX(Yi−Yˉ)sYr = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i\frac{(X_i-\bar X)}{s_X}\frac{(Y_i-\bar Y)}{s_Y}r=n−11​∑i​sX​(Xi​−Xˉ)​sY​(Yi​−Yˉ)​ .
  • Population correlation ρ=E[(X−μX)σx(Y−μY)σY]\rho = E[\frac{(X-\mu_X)}{\sigma_x}\frac{(Y-\mu_Y)}{\sigma_Y}]ρ=E[σx​(X−μX​)​σY​(Y−μY​)​]

Z-score

Given XiX_iXi​ , define

This follows the standard normal distribution. Namely Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim {\cal N}(0,1)Z∼N(0,1) .

T-score

Given XiX_iXi​ , define

This follows the Student’s t distribution with DoF (n−1)(n-1)(n−1) . Namely, t∼T(n−1)t \sim T(n-1)t∼T(n−1)

2-sided CI

The α\alphaα-confidence interval for μ\muμ, given Xˉ\bar XXˉ is (XL,XH)(X_L,X_H)(XL​,XH​) where

Using Z-score here, but same can be used for any kind of score.

1-sided CI

Given Xˉ\bar XXˉ and σ\sigmaσ, the left-tailed one-sided interval for μ\muμ is (−∞,XH](-\infty,X_H](−∞,XH​] where

This is reported after rejecting the null hypothesis in a left tailed one-sided (Z) test.

Similarly, [XL,∞)[X_L,\infty)[XL​,∞) is used after rejecting H0H_0H0​ for a right-tailed one-sided test.

Hypothesis testing

Given XiX_iXi​, compute some test-statistic SSS from XiX_iXi​ with (or approaching) a known distribution. Suppose the value comes out to be sss.

The alternate hypothesis HAH_AHA​ is something you are proposing.

The null hypothesis H0=¬HAH_0 = \neg H_AH0​=¬HA​ is the exact opposite of HAH_AHA​ .

Two sided test :

  • H0:E[S]=0H_0 : E[S] = 0H0​:E[S]=0
  • HA:E[S]≠0H_A : E[S] \ne 0HA​:E[S]=0

Left-tailed one sided test :

  • H0:E[S]≥0H_0 : E[S] \ge 0H0​:E[S]≥0
  • HA:E[S]<0H_A : E[S] < 0HA​:E[S]<0

Similar for right-tailed one-sided test.

Define:

  • significance level α=P(reject H0 ∣ H0)\alpha = P(\text{reject }H_0\,|\, H_0)α=P(reject H0​∣H0​) is probability of committing a type I error (aka rejection error)
  • β=P(fail to reject H0 ∣ HA)\beta = P(\text{fail to reject } H_0\,|\,H_A )β=P(fail to reject H0​∣HA​) is probability of committing a type II error.
    This is function of α,H0\alpha,H_0α,H0​ (giving critical values for SSS) and HAH_AHA​ (giving distribution of SSS over which to compute probability)
  • 1−β=P(reject H ∣ HA)1-\beta = P(\text{reject } H\,|\,H_A)1−β=P(reject H∣HA​) is the power of the test.
  • p=P(S more extreme than s ∣ H0)p= P(S \text{ more extreme than } s \,|\,H_0)p=P(S more extreme than s∣H0​) is the p-value.
Power

Suppose H0:μ≤μ0H_0 : \mu \le \mu_0H0​:μ≤μ0​ and HA:μ>μ0H_A : \mu > \mu_0HA​:μ>μ0​ and HAH_AHA​ is true (ground truth). In particular, μ=μ1\mu = \mu_1μ=μ1​ , then

The curve of 1−β1-\beta1−β vs μ1\mu_1μ1​ is called a Power Curve.

The distributions of Xˉ\bar XXˉ (as a r.v.) under H0H_0H0​ and H1H_1H1​ get sharper on increasing nnn, thus decreasing β\betaβ (for a fixed α\alphaα).

Thus, the power increases with nnn. Suppose the minimum required power is 1−βmax1-\beta_{max}1−βmax​ . Then, we have

But note that zβmax=−z(1−βmax)z_{\beta_{max}} = -z_{(1-\beta_{max})}zβmax​​=−z(1−βmax​)​. Thus, we have

For a two sided test, we have

So, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for β≤βmax\beta \le \beta_{max}β≤βmax​ is

Thus, we get this sufficient condition :

ANOVA

Given X1,i1,X2,i2,X3,i3…Xk,ikX_{1,i_1},X_{2,i_2},X_{3,i_3}\dots X_{k,i_k}X1,i1​​,X2,i2​​,X3,i3​​…Xk,ik​​ , namely kkk samples of sizes n1,n2,…nkn_1,n_2,\dots n_kn1​,n2​,…nk​ respectively, we want to know whether the samples are “very” different from each other.

The null hypothesis H0:μj=μ  ∀j∈[k]H_0:\mu_j = \mu\;\forall j\in[k]H0​:μj​=μ∀j∈[k] says that all samples come from distributions with same means.

The alternate hypothesis is then HA:∃j1,j2 s.t. μj1≠μj2H_A : \exists j_1,j_2 \text{ s.t. } \mu_{j_1} \ne \mu_{j_2}HA​:∃j1​,j2​ s.t. μj1​​=μj2​​

Define

Defining N=∑jnjN = \sum_j n_jN=∑j​nj​ , we can rewrite this mess as :

The test-statistic fff follows the F-distribution with numerator DoF as k−1k-1k−1 and denominator DoF as N−kN-kN−k .

According to the alternate hypothesis, sB2≫sW2s_B^2 \gg s_W^2sB2​≫sW2​ and thus f≫1f \gg 1f≫1 .

Multiple comparisons

Given data X1,i1,X2,i2…Xk,ikX_{1,i_1},X_{2,i_2}\dots X_{k,i_k}X1,i1​​,X2,i2​​…Xk,ik​​ we can run (k2)\binom{k}{2}(2k​) two-sample tests to test for

Each two sample test will have null hypothesis H0,i,j:μi=μjH_{0,i,j} : \mu_i = \mu_jH0,i,j​:μi​=μj​ and alternate hypothesis HA,i,j:μi≠μjH_{A,i,j} : \mu_i \ne \mu_jHA,i,j​:μi​=μj​. If any of these (k2)\binom k 2(2k​) end up with rejection of the null hypothesis, then we reject H0H_0H0​ .

Here we can also take advantage of the fact that H0H_0 H0​ states that all the distributions are similar (and thus have equal variances) and use sWs_WsW​ as the pooled standard deviation. Thus the t-statistic is

Suppose we set significance level α′\alpha'α′ for each two-sample test, which is P(reject H0,i,j ∣ H0,i,j)P(\text{reject } H_{0,i,j}\,|\,H_{0,i,j})P(reject H0,i,j​∣H0,i,j​) then the significance level for the main test, namely P(reject H0 ∣ H0)=1−P(not reject H0 ∣ H0)P(\text{reject } H_0\,|\,H_0) = 1-P(\text{not reject } H_0 \,|\, H_0)P(reject H0​∣H0​)=1−P(not reject H0​∣H0​) is given by

Now, Bernoulli’s inequality states that for any x≥−1x \ge -1x≥−1 and n∈Wn\in \mathbb{W}n∈W , we have (1+x)n≥1+nx(1+x)^n \ge 1+nx(1+x)n≥1+nx . Since −α′≥−1-\alpha' \ge -1−α′≥−1 , we can now write,

So, if we want α<αmax\alpha < \alpha_{max}α<αmax​, the actual significance level set before data collection, we can set

This is called Bonnefori correction.

Sign test

In case the sample size is too small (so we can’t use CLT) and the distributions are not normal, as per some normality test (e.g. Anderson’ test), we do non-parametric tests.

The sign test is the non-parametric analogue of the paired sample t-test.

Define the “positive” function as

Given [X1,i X2,i]i[X_{1,i}\,X_{2,i}]_i[X1,i​X2,i​]i​ , define di=X1,i−X2,id_i = X_{1,i} - X_{2,i}di​=X1,i​−X2,i​ . Let δmedian\delta_\text{median}δmedian​ be the population median of the distribution of the random variable ddd. We will be testing

Now, define

Note that pos(di)\text{pos}(d_i)pos(di​) are i.i.d.r.v . Moreover, under the null hypothesis, since the median difference is 0, it means that P(d>0∣d≠0)=P(d<0∣d≠0)=1/2P(d > 0 | d\ne 0) = P(d < 0| d\ne 0) = 1/2P(d>0∣d=0)=P(d<0∣d=0)=1/2 . Thus, pos(di)\text{pos}(d_i)pos(di​) are a bernoulli i.i.d.r.v with p=1/2p = 1/2p=1/2 , making DDD a binomial distribution. Namely, we have

So, we define the Z-score

When nn n is large, this comes from an approximate standard normal distribution and we can use ZZZ to compute the p-value as 2(1−Φ(∣Z∣))2(1-\Phi(|Z|))2(1−Φ(∣Z∣)) .

When nnn is small, we can compute the p-value as

Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

This is an upgraded version of the sign test

Given [X1,i,X2,i]i[X_{1,i},X_{2,i}]_i[X1,i​,X2,i​]i​ , compute di=Xi,i−X2,id_i = X_{i,i} - X_{2,i}di​=Xi,i​−X2,i​ . Remove all data where di=0d_i = 0di​=0, giving us the actual sample size n=∣{di ∣ di≠0}∣n = |\{d_i\,|\,d_i \ne 0\}|n=∣{di​∣di​=0}∣ .

Then, rank the absolute differences ∣di∣|d_i|∣di​∣ and break ties using average of ranks.

Thus, for every did_idi​ , we will get a rank ri∈[1,n]r_i \in [1,n]ri​∈[1,n] .

Now, define

Assuming no tie-breaking, we can rewrite this as

Here dird_{i_{r}}dir​​ is the difference value with rank rrr .

Now, pos(dir)\text{pos}(d_{i_{r}})pos(dir​​) are i.i.d.r.v and under H0H_0H0​, they are beroulli variables with success probability 1/2 .

Thus, we can write

With a bit of cleaning up, we can write :

When nnn is large, Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim N(0,1)Z∼N(0,1) .

Note that we could also use neg\text{neg}neg instead of pos\text{pos}pos to define TTT . Usually, out of neg,pos\text{neg},\text{pos}neg,pos, the one which gives smaller TTT is used.

Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test

Given X1,iX_{1,i}X1,i​ and X2,jX_{2,j}X2,j​ , combine them into a single array and rank them (ties get average ranks).

Thus, for each X1,iX_{1,i}X1,i​ , we have a rank r1,ir_{1,i}r1,i​ and for each X2,jX_{2,j}X2,j​ we have a rank r2,jr_{2,j}r2,j​ .

Sum the ranks to compute rank-sums for both groups.

Denote the smaller of the rank-sums as WWW. We can, WLOG assume is is from first sample.

Let IrI_rIr​ be an indicator function which is 1 if the rthr^{th}rth rank is in the first sample and 0 if it is in second.Thus, we have

Since expectation doesn’t need independence to distribute, we can write

As for the variance, I don’t know how this is derived, but we have

Using all this, we define

This Z-score is for testing these two :

where m1,m2m_1,m_2m1​,m2​ are the median values of the population distributions.

Kruskal Walis Test

This is similar to Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test.

Given X1,i1,X2,i2…Xk,ikX_{1,i_1},X_{2,i_2}\dots X_{k,i_k}X1,i1​​,X2,i2​​…Xk,ik​​ , combine it all in one array and rank (average rank for ties). Then, for each sample jjj compute the rank-sum RjR_jRj​.

Define n=∑jnjn = \sum_j n_jn=∑j​nj​ as the sum of all sizes.

Now, we move to the messed up part.

This test statistic has the χ2\chi^2χ2 distribution with k−1k-1k−1 degrees of freedom.

This will be used to test these :

Pearson Correlation Test

Given Xi,YiX_i,Y_iXi​,Yi​ , if we want to know if there is significant (positive or negative) correlation between their underlying distribution, namely HA:ρ≠0H_A : \rho \ne 0HA​:ρ=0 , we will first compute the Pearson Correlation Coefficient as

Then, using that, we will compute the test statistic

This has a Student’s t distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom. Using this, we will test

Spearman Rank Correlation

The spearman rank correlation is again an estimator for ρ\rhoρ. We can compute it like this :

  • Rank XiX_iXi​ separately to get ranks AiA_iAi​.
  • Rank YiY_iYi​ to get ranks BiB_iBi​
  • Then, find the Pearson correlation coefficient of these ranks to get rsr_srs​ (the spearman rank correlation)

The third step can be made more efficient with some algebraic manipulation resulting in

To test whether there is significant correlation ρ\rhoρ, we once again convert this to a test statistic as

This, again follows the Student’s t distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom.

Regression

Note that this isn’t necessarily linear regression.

Suppose that you are given dependent and independent variables xi,yix_{i},y_ixi​,yi​ such that the underlying r.v. X,YX,YX,Y have a bi-variate normal distribution for the joint P.D.F, then for each iii, you would create a prediction y^i=Y^(xi)\hat y_i = \hat Y(x_i)y^​i​=Y^(xi​) . These will give this estimate of the mean square prediction error EY[(Y−Y^(X))2]E_Y[(Y-\hat Y(X))^2]EY​[(Y−Y^(X))2] :

The quantity sy∣xs_{y|x}sy∣x​ is often called the “standard deviation from regression”

The value kkk is the number of parameters in the prediction model. For example, for simple linear regression, this is 2.

It should however be noted that the model will never be “perfect” and thus

The right hand side, namely the MSPE is actually what we are estimating with sY∣X2s^2_{Y|X}sY∣X2​.

Thus, as the model performs worse, we can expect sY∣X2s^2_{Y|X}sY∣X2​ to increase. And this quantity decreases :

This is called the “coefficient of determination”.

For simple linear regression, we somehow have the identity R2=r2R^2 = r^2R2=r2 (no stats involved… this is mathematically true).

Homoskedacity (HS)

Homoskedacity is the phenomenon when the variance of a variable YYY when conditioned by X=xX=xX=x, namely Var(Y∣x)\text{Var}(Y|x)Var(Y∣x) is constant w.r.t. xxx. Note that E[Y∣x]E[Y|x]E[Y∣x] does change with xxx.

In such a scenario, one can say that

Now, we can write E[Y2∣X]=Var(Y∣X)+(E[Y∣X])2E[Y^2|X] = \text{Var}(Y|X) + (E[Y|X])^2E[Y2∣X]=Var(Y∣X)+(E[Y∣X])2 .

Now, since Var(Y∣X)=σY∣X2\text{Var}(Y|X) =\sigma^2_{Y|X}Var(Y∣X)=σY∣X2​ is constant, we can write

Bivariate Normal distribution (BND)

Now, for a bivariate normal distribution (BND), we have this identity :

Plugging it in the final equation we got for HS, we get

Note that BND automatically implies HS.

Simple Linear Regression (SLR)

Estimating σY∣X\sigma_{Y|X}σY∣X​ by sY∣Xs_{Y|X}sY∣X​, we can get estimates se^(β^1),se^(β^0)\hat {\text{se}}(\hat \beta_1),\hat {\text{se}}(\hat \beta_0)se^(β^​1​),se^(β^​0​).

Testing SLR models

Suppose someone came up with a linear model

using some means other than direct estimation of β^0,β^1\hat \beta_0,\hat \beta_1β^​0​,β^​1​ from the data xi,yix_i,y_ixi​,yi​ .

You have reason to believe that this theoretical model Y^T\hat Y_TY^T​ doesn’t work and want to prove that using the data. Just proving that one of the parameters is wrong is enough. In particular, you have:

You already have estimates β^0,β^1\hat \beta_0,\hat \beta_1β^​0​,β^​1​ from the data for β0,β1\beta_0,\beta_1β0​,β1​ similar to how we have the estimate Xˉ\bar XXˉ for μ\muμ .

You also have estimates se^(β^0),se^(β^1)\hat{\text{se}}(\hat \beta_0),\hat{\text{se}}(\hat \beta_1)se^(β^​0​),se^(β^​1​) for se(β^0),se(β^1)\text{se}(\hat \beta_0),\text{se}(\hat \beta_1)se(β^​0​),se(β^​1​) similar to how we use (s/n)(s/\sqrt n)(s/n​) for (σ/n)=se(Xˉ)(\sigma/\sqrt n) = \text{se}(\bar X)(σ/n​)=se(Xˉ) in a t-test.

With all this, we can compute the t-statistics

Under H0,0,H0,1H_{0,0},H_{0,1}H0,0​,H0,1​ both of these have the t-distribution with n−2n-2n−2 degrees of freedom.

Setting β00=β10=0\beta_{00} = \beta_{10} = 0β00​=β10​=0 makes this into a significance test where you are checking whether the terms are even needed. In that scenario, we have

But remember that for simple linear regression,

This gives us

which is the same expression we got for the correlation test.
